entries.title: COMMENTARY ON THE APOCALYPSE 22:3 on revelation 22:3
entries.excerpt: Anathema can be interpreted in a twofold manner. It either refers to that which is holy for many but is dedicated to God alone, or it refers to that which is sacred to the whole of creation and to the holy powers but is attributed to the devil because of his utter alienation and separation from that which is good. We think that here katathema is used in an emphatic sense. For such a thing is not dedicated to anyone, but it is "put aside" for the devil, being subordinated to him and condemned along with him. Such a thing will not exist within that city.

entries.title: TRACTATE ON THE APOCALYPSE 22:3-4 on revelation 22:3
entries.excerpt: There will be [in that city] nothing accursed, that is, no temptation will approach, no profane sin shall be recorded through the rashness of the envious one. Rather, in its midst will be the rule of the divine power and of the Lamb. All will serve him with the exaltation of that city's happiness. They shall rejoice in the vision of his face and in the sweetness of his presence, and they shall bear his name engraved in the sign of his cross on their foreheads.

entries.title: Commentary on Revelation on revelation 22:3
entries.excerpt:
And he says there will be no more curse. For although the anathemas now may flee vehemently, nevertheless we are comforted in them, with myriad excuses sprouting up in us in knowledge and indeed in ignorance; but then the saints will be free from every stain of the soul, just as they are from physical stain.

And he says that in the city will be the kingdom of God. For this is the throne. And he says the saints will serve him with worship not burdensome, but with spiritual pleasure and joy.

entries.title: COMMENTARY ON THE APOCALYPSE 22:3 on revelation 22:3
entries.excerpt: He does not speak here of thrones. For where there is a natural and undifferentiated unity, there the glory of the Godhead may be especially esteemed. There is one throne of an indivisible rule that cannot be divided by the cunning of Arian depravity or confounded by the doubts of Sabellian vanity. Rather, it is able to be contemplated only by the reception of catholic truth and is proven to be rightly held by the confession of her faith.
